summary: I was inside a hangar when I first heard that one of the World Trade Center towers had been struck. We gathered around a large screen TV and watched live as the second plane hit the other tower. It was clear to all of us that this was no accident but a terrorist attack. This happened early on September 11, 2001, and it set the tone for what was about to unfold in Washington, D.C.

When the World Trade Center was hit in New York City, we immediately thought that Washington, D.C. might be next. The attacks in New York City were the first sign that something major was happening on September 11, 2001. It was a pivotal moment that made us prepare for possible emergencies here in the capital.

After the attacks in New York City, we were on high alert in Washington, D.C. We prepared our helicopters and responded quickly when the Pentagon was hit. The city was chaotic but controlled, with many agencies working together. We even took over air traffic control temporarily when Reagan Tower had to evacuate due to smoke.

I arrived at the Pentagon within about ten minutes after it was struck on September 11, 2001. The scene was smoky and chaotic but controlled, with people evacuating and emergency responders rushing in. We landed about 150 yards from the impact site and helped evacuate severely burned patients. The damage was initially hard to see but worsened as walls started caving in.

Reagan National Airport played a critical role on September 11, 2001. We received a crash alert from Reagan Tower about a possible plane going down near the Pentagon. Later, Reagan Tower had to evacuate because smoke from the Pentagon was affecting their air system, and they transferred air traffic control responsibilities to our helicopter unit. Reagan Tower also provided vital updates on unauthorized aircraft in the area.

Eagle One was the first helicopter to launch and arrive at the Pentagon on September 11, 2001. It stayed airborne to control the airspace and coordinate the response, including calling in other aviation units. Eagle One also took over air traffic control when Reagan Tower evacuated and later repositioned to a safer location near the Pentagon. The crew consisted of one pilot and two rescue technicians.

I was aboard Eagle Two on September 11, 2001, which was the second helicopter to respond to the Pentagon attack. Our crew included two pilots, myself and the unit commander, plus two medical personnel from the Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences. We landed near the Pentagon and helped evacuate severely burned patients to the Washington Hospital Center. Eagle Two also returned to the area later to assist with surveillance missions for the FBI and Department of Defense.

entity: Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences
summary: On September 11, 2001, two medical personnel from the Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences were aboard Eagle Two during the Pentagon response. They provided critical medical support during patient evacuation. Their presence was vital in managing the severely burned patients we transported to the hospital.

The Washington Hospital Center, often called MedStar, was the trauma unit where we transported severely burned patients from the Pentagon on September 11, 2001. After landing near the Pentagon, we flew patients there quickly to receive emergency care. The hospital was a key part of the medical response during those critical hours.

MedStar Health is the private company operating the trauma unit at the Washington Hospital Center. On September 11, 2001, we coordinated with MedStar to transport and treat patients injured in the Pentagon attack. Their air ambulance services were requested to assist with the medevac operations alongside our helicopters and other agencies.

The Fairfax County Police Department was one of the agencies requested to respond with their medevac helicopters on September 11, 2001. We worked closely with them during the Pentagon emergency to coordinate patient transport and airspace control. Their cooperation was essential in managing the multiple aircraft involved in the response.

The West Virginia State Police, referred to as Mountain State Police, were called to assist with medevac operations on September 11, 2001. They brought their aircraft to support the response efforts around the Pentagon. We had a good working relationship with them and relied on their capabilities during the crisis.

The District of Columbia Police Department was asked to bring their helicopter to control airspace during the Pentagon emergency on September 11, 2001. Eagle One was initially controlling the air, but the D.C. Police took over to allow our helicopters to focus on medevac missions. Their involvement helped maintain order in the restricted airspace around Washington, D.C.

After the immediate Pentagon response on September 11, 2001, the FBI requested our helicopter unit to conduct multiple surveillance flights over the damaged area. We provided live video downlinks to ground units to help assess the damage and monitor the site. Working with the FBI was crucial for security and investigation efforts in the aftermath of the attack.

The United States Department of Defense coordinated with us on September 11, 2001, requesting aerial surveillance flights over the Pentagon after the attack. We provided live video feeds to assist their assessment of the damage and security operations. Our helicopter missions were an important part of their response and recovery efforts that day.

On September 11, 2001, I witnessed the unfolding terrorist attacks starting with the World Trade Center in New York City and then the Pentagon in Washington, D.C. We responded immediately with our helicopters, Eagle One and Eagle Two, to assist with medical evacuations and airspace control. The day was chaotic but marked by coordinated efforts among multiple agencies to manage the crisis and save lives.

During the September 11 attacks, we were informed by Reagan Tower that an unauthorized aircraft was heading toward Washington, D.C., which we later learned was the plane that went down near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. This information helped us understand the broader scope of the attacks and the restricted airspace situation. Pittsburgh became part of the unfolding story as the crash site of the fourth hijacked plane.
